items.question_text: Under 9-7-1, when must pertinent information about parachute operations be coordinated with other affected ATC facilities or sectors?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-1, coordination occurs both prior to and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps that begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction.

choices.text: Only when the jump aircraft enters Class B airspace.
Prior to and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps that begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction.
Only at the end of the jump operation, after all parachutists have landed.
Only before the first jump of the day, with no requirement to coordinate again until the next day.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-1, what geographic trigger determines whether coordination is required for a parachute jump or series of jumps?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-1, the trigger is whether the jump or series of jumps begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction. If either the start or finish is in your area, coordination is required.

choices.text: Whether the jump aircraft departs from an airport within your area of jurisdiction.
Whether the jump or series of jumps begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction.
Whether the parachutists land on an airport you control.
Whether the jump aircraft transits your airspace en route to the drop zone.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-1, what 14 CFR section prescribes the information required from persons requesting authorization for nonemergency parachute jumping?
items.explanation: The note references 14 CFR section 105.15 for the information required from each person requesting authorization or submitting notification for nonemergency parachute jumping activity.

choices.text: 14 CFR section 105.15.
14 CFR section 101.39.
14 CFR section 105.25.
14 CFR section 91.137.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-2, where must parachute operations in Class A, B, or C airspace be authorized?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-2a, authorize parachute operations only within airspace designated for the jumping activity.

choices.text: Anywhere within the controlling facility's Class A, B, or C boundary.
Only within airspace designated for the jumping activity.
Only above 10,000 feet MSL regardless of airspace designation.
Anywhere the jump aircraft can maintain two-way radio communications.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-2, what must be done with aircraft that are NOT participating in the jump operation?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-2b, separate aircraft other than those participating in the jump operation from the airspace authorized for the jumping activity.

choices.text: Separate them from the airspace authorized for the jumping activity.
Issue traffic advisories only, because separation is not required around designated jump airspace in Class A, B, or C.
Allow them to transit the area if the pilot accepts responsibility for visual separation.
Apply separation only if the nonparticipating aircraft is IFR.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-2c, what authority does ATC have regarding parachute operations in Class A, B, or C airspace?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-2c, ATC may impose any conditions and restrictions which in the controller's judgment would promote the safety of the operation.

choices.text: ATC may impose any conditions and restrictions that would promote safety of the operation, as judged by the controller.
ATC may only impose restrictions specifically pre-approved in a Letter of Agreement with the jump operator.
ATC has no authority to impose additional conditions beyond those already in 14 CFR Part 105.
ATC may impose restrictions only after coordinating with the Regional Office.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-3, what type of airspace does this paragraph apply to for parachute operations?
items.explanation: 9-7-3 applies to Class D airspace in which there is a functioning control tower. It is labeled TERMINAL.

choices.text: Class D airspace in which there is a functioning control tower.
All Class D airspace regardless of tower operations.
Class D and Class E airspace with an operating control tower.
Any controlled airspace where a terminal radar facility is available.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-3, how is parachute jumping authorized in Class D airspace?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-3a, authorize with respect to known or observed traffic. This differs from 9-7-2's requirement to separate nonparticipating aircraft from designated airspace.

choices.text: With respect to known or observed traffic.
Automatically upon request, because Class D towers must approve all parachute operations.
Only after all nonparticipating aircraft have left the Class D airspace.
By establishing a temporary restricted area around the drop zone.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-3, to whom must advisory information be issued for Class D parachute operations?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-3b, issue advisory information to the jump aircraft AND to nonparticipating aircraft as necessary for the safe conduct of the jump operation.

choices.text: Only the jump aircraft, because nonparticipating aircraft are separated by the tower.
The jump aircraft and nonparticipating aircraft, as necessary.
Only nonparticipating aircraft, because the jump aircraft already knows its own operation.
Only aircraft on the tower frequency, excluding those on ground control.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-3, how does the Class D parachute authorization standard differ from the Class A/B/C standard in 9-7-2?
items.explanation: 9-7-2 (Class A/B/C) requires designating airspace for the jump AND separating nonparticipating aircraft from that airspace. 9-7-3 (Class D) uses 'with respect to known or observed traffic' and issues advisories without the same separation mandate. The Class D standard is less restrictive.

choices.text: Class D uses 'with respect to known or observed traffic'; Class A/B/C requires designating specific airspace and separating nonparticipating aircraft from it.
There is no difference; both paragraphs use identical standards for all controlled airspace.
Class D is more restrictive because it requires an active control tower to approve all operations.
Class A/B/C uses advisory-only standards; Class D requires full separation of all traffic.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-4, what three elements must be included in the traffic advisory to the jump aircraft before a Class E parachute jump?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-4a, include aircraft type, altitude, and direction of flight of all known traffic that will transit the airspace within which the jump will be conducted.

choices.text: Aircraft type, altitude, and direction of flight of all known traffic that will transit the jump airspace.
Location, time, and duration of the jump.
Jump aircraft callsign, number of jumpers, and exit altitude.
Wind direction, cloud ceiling, and visibility at the drop zone.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-4, what four elements must advisories to nonparticipating aircraft include for Class E parachute operations?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-4b, advisories must consist of the location, time, duration, and altitude from which the jump will be made.

choices.text: Location, time, duration, and altitude from which the jump will be made.
Jump aircraft type, altitude, direction, and speed.
Location, altitude, aircraft type, and number of jumpers.
Time, duration, wind conditions, and recovery area.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-4, when individual transmissions to nonparticipating aircraft are impractical, what alternative methods may be used?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-4c, advisories may be broadcast on appropriate control frequencies or, when available, the ATIS broadcast.

choices.text: Broadcast on appropriate control frequencies or, when available, the ATIS broadcast.
File a NOTAM and stop all individual transmissions immediately.
Transmit only on guard frequency (121.5) to avoid congesting ATC frequencies.
Skip advisories to nonparticipating traffic and only advise the jump aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-4, what 14 CFR requirement applies to radio communications before parachute jumping in Class E airspace?
items.explanation: Per 14 CFR 105.13 (referenced in the note), communications must be established at least 5 minutes before jumping begins, for the purpose of receiving information about known air traffic in the vicinity.

choices.text: 14 CFR 105.13 requires radio contact at least 5 minutes before jumping begins to receive traffic information, unless otherwise authorized by ATC.
14 CFR 105.13 requires radio contact only at the moment of jump exit.
14 CFR 105.13 requires radio contact 10 minutes before the aircraft reaches the drop zone.
14 CFR 105.13 waives all radio requirements for Class E parachute operations.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-4d, when a pilot requests assistance avoiding the jump airspace, what is the controller's obligation?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-4d, when requested by the pilot and to the extent possible, assist nonparticipating aircraft to avoid the airspace within which the jump will be conducted.

choices.text: Assist to the extent possible when requested by the pilot.
Automatically vector all nonparticipating aircraft away from the jump airspace without pilot request.
Refuse all avoidance requests because jump airspace cannot be modified once the operation is authorized.
Only assist IFR aircraft; VFR pilots must avoid the area on their own.

items.question_text: Under 9-7-4, fill in the blank: Advisories to nonparticipating aircraft must consist of the _____, time, _____, and altitude from which the jump will be made.
items.explanation: The four elements are location, time, duration, and altitude — all specifically required by 9-7-4b.

choices.text: location / duration
heading / speed
aircraft type / frequency
jump zone / weather

items.situation: A parachute operation is requested in Class C airspace. The jump operator wants to use an area that is within the Class C boundary but was not previously designated for jumping. A trainee says you can authorize it anywhere in Class C because the airspace is controlled.
items.question_text: May you authorize the jump in undesignated airspace within Class C?

choices.text: No. 9-7-2a requires authorizing parachute operations only within airspace designated for the jumping activity, not anywhere within Class C.
Yes. Any airspace within the Class C boundary qualifies as designated for jumping activity once the tower approves it.
Yes. If the jump aircraft can maintain radio contact, the entire Class C surface area is considered designated.
No. Class C airspace may never be used for parachute operations under any circumstances.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-2a, authorize parachute operations only within airspace designated for the jumping activity. Class C boundaries are not the same as designated jump airspace.
items.para_context: CLASS A, CLASS B, AND CLASS C AIRSPACE

items.situation: A jump operator requests parachute operations into your Class D airspace. Two VFR aircraft are in the pattern and a third is inbound. A trainee wants to approve the jump immediately without giving any advisories because the jump aircraft has the traffic in sight.
items.question_text: Is immediate approval without advisories correct under 9-7-3?

choices.text: No. 9-7-3 requires authorizing the jump with respect to known or observed traffic and issuing advisory information as necessary to both the jump aircraft and nonparticipating aircraft.
Yes. If the jump pilot reports traffic in sight, the tower may approve the jump without further advisories.
Yes. Class D towers are only required to issue advisories to IFR aircraft; VFR pattern traffic handles separation visually.
No. Jump operations are prohibited in Class D airspace when any other aircraft are in the pattern.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-3, authorize with respect to known or observed traffic and issue advisory information to both the jump aircraft and nonparticipating aircraft as necessary.

items.situation: A jump aircraft calls 2 minutes before planned exit in Class E airspace. Several aircraft will transit the area. A trainee says the jump can proceed because 2 minutes is reasonable notice, and advisories to nonparticipating aircraft can wait until after the jump.
items.question_text: What issues exist with this approach under 9-7-4 and 14 CFR 105.13?

choices.text: 14 CFR 105.13 requires radio contact at least 5 minutes before jumping to receive traffic information. 9-7-4 also requires pre-jump advisories to the jump aircraft and advisories to known nonparticipating aircraft before the jump.
There are no issues. 2 minutes is sufficient for Class E jumps and advisories to nonparticipating traffic can follow the jump.
Only the 5-minute rule is an issue; advisories to nonparticipating aircraft are optional in Class E.
Neither the 5-minute rule nor nonparticipating advisories apply — those are only for Class D operations.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-4a and 14 CFR 105.13, the jump aircraft must establish radio contact at least 5 minutes before jumping begins to receive traffic information, and advisories to the jump aircraft and nonparticipating aircraft are required before the jump.
